title: Python Programming
text:
General Information
Welcome to the home of the Python Programming Module of the Computational Biology Course. In this module we will learn the basics of Python Programming, and give and introduction to Python for Data Analytics and Statistical Analysis, using a variety of specialized Python packages. The module does not requires any previous programming knowledge, although this can certainly be helpful. A previous knowledge of basic concepts of statistics (descriptive statistics, distributions, simple hypothesis testing) is required, and has been provide in the Statistics module of this course. The Python and R programming languages share many common concepts and methods, and students can certainly leverage the newly acquired R skills in their Python learning. In order to facilitate the transition, an R to Python comparative table is available



Teachjng Approach
This is an hands-on course, centered on the student activities with guidance from the teacher. The teaching sessions use Colab Notebooks, a coding environement create bv Google that requires minimal setup from the students part. The course consists of a series of Colab Notebooks that the students will go through, replicating calculations and solving simple coding challenges. Refer to the Notebooks Page to see the full and general instructions on how to use.



Module Evaluation
Student performance is this module will be evaluated by means of an exam done using the on-line tutorial system at University of Algarve (moodle based). This exam will simulaneously evaluate the Statistic and the Python Programming modules. There will be both mutiple choice questions and questions were students will be asked to write a small piece of code to perform a particular task (students will be allowed to access a table with Python commands).
The PhD students enrolling this course will be subject to an additional evaluation step. After finishing the exam, they will be given a simple data analysis exercise and be requested to produce a Colab Notebook with the analysis and explanatory comments. The Notebooks so created will be shared with the teacher for evaluation.

title: Notebooks
text: The page lists the Google Colab Notebooks used in this course. In order to use them, students need to have an active Google account. By clikcing on one of the links below, the students will open a copy of the notebook on their browsers (Crhome is recommend, but it should work in any browser). Since their are viewing a notebook created by another person (the teacher, in this case), they need to use the option “File , Save a Copy in Drive” to create a copy of the document in their Google Drive. After that, any edits on the notebook will be preserved. After that, the notebook needs to be activated, that is, it need to be bound to a virtual machine running on the Google cloud. In this way, students will be offered a full computing environment without the need to install any software on their computers. Keep in mind, though, that any file create on the virtual machine will erased after the machine disconnects, so anything to keep must be either downloaded or copied to the Google drive. To activete the notebook, presse on the “Connect” button on the upper right side.

title: Software
text:
The use of Colab Notebooks for teaching make the software requirements essentially non-existent. However, some students may want to locally install the Python ecosystem on their local machines, for various reasons:

You may want to code from scripts directly, instead of using the cell based philosophy of the colab notebooks. Managing complex code and data pipellins becomes tricky on a notebook environment.
The Colab Environment is free, but that comes with a price: the virtual machine running your code is very light on resources (both CPU, RAM and disk space) and for heavy computing and very complex tasks will be impossible to use.
You many need to install packages that are not compatible with the colab virtual machine environment, or very difficult to set up.
You may require live access to use repositories of data, and to write and write massive amounts of data

For all these reasons, you may need to install and run Python locally. Two comon ways tio do it:

Install python directly from www.python.org - this is simple and provies you with a basic environent and and IDE
Install Conda or Miniconda from www.anaconda.com - you get a much powerful environment, with access to virtually thousands of packages and the benefit of isolated environment with much less danger of creating conflicts with other software installed on your machine. Trick to install packages are very often much easier to install in Conda than on a bare Python environment.
If you are on a Linux or Mac machine, you already have python installed, you just need to install packages (like numpy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn or scipy) beyond what is installed by default on these systems. You can install these packages in virtual environemtns to isolate different software requeriments and distributions.

In case you prefer a local installation, but you still want to be working in a notebook-base environment like Colab (very common among data scientists), you can install Jupyter under you Python installation to be able to create, save and run Python Notebooks (Colab Notebooks are in fact Jupyter Notebooks with some Google tweaking).
The teacher can provide help, should you decide for a local installation.
